name: Peak-End Rule (Kahneman) + Onboarding/Churn Application
definition_origin: Daniel Kahneman, Barbara Fredrickson, Charles Schreiber, and Donald Redelmeier (1993) — 'When more pain is preferred to less: Adding a better end' (Psychological Science). Extended by Kahneman & Redelmeier (1996) on colonoscopy patients showing memory of duration is replaced by memory of peak intensity and ending. Codified in Kahneman's 'Thinking, Fast and Slow' (2011) as the experiencing-self vs. remembering-self distinction. Applied to UX by Nielsen Norman Group (Lexie Kane, December 2018, 'The Peak-End Rule: How Impressions Become Memories').
core_mechanism: Human memory does not record duration accurately ('duration neglect') and instead summarizes an experience as a weighted average of its emotional peak (best or worst moment) plus its ending state. The remembering-self — not the experiencing-self — drives whether a user returns. Therefore total experience minutes matter less for retention than (a) at least one above-baseline emotional peak somewhere during the session, and (b) a positive ending. A flat 'pretty good' session is worse than one with a real spike + a satisfying close, even if the spike-version had more friction in the middle.
neuroscience_correlate: Memory consolidation is biased toward emotionally salient events (amygdala-hippocampus interaction; LaBar & Cabeza 2006). The remembering-self/experiencing-self distinction maps loosely to default-mode-network (autobiographical recall) vs. moment-to-moment limbic/sensory processing. Dopaminergic prediction error at the ending may explain why a positive close is over-weighted in retrospective evaluation. Direct fMRI evidence for the peak-end rule itself is limited.
empirical_evidence: (1) Kahneman, Fredrickson, Schreiber & Redelmeier (1993) — cold-pressor study, n=32, participants preferred a longer painful trial that ended with milder pain over a shorter trial that ended at peak pain. (2) Redelmeier & Kahneman (1996) — colonoscopy patients (n=154 in one arm, n=109 in another), patients in the elongated 'softer ending' condition rated the procedure as less unpleasant and were more likely to return for follow-up screening. (3) Frontiers in Psychology 2019 (Müller, Sirianni, Addessi) — confirmed peak-end robustness across complex heterogeneous experiences. (4) Mental health context replication — PMC11343653 (2024) review confirms peak-end applies to depression rumination, therapy session memory, and digital-health adherence. (5) UX industry validation — NN/g (2018, Lexie Kane) and Laws of UX both treat peak-end as foundational; Duolingo, Headspace, and TurboTax cited as canonical applications.
measurable_outcome_metric: Day-1 → Day-7 → Day-30 retention curves, NPS at session end vs. mid-session, repeat-session rate, user-reported satisfaction / 5-star review rate, churn rate after first negative-peak event, willingness to repeat task (Kahneman's original DV), share / referral rate immediately post-session.
engineering_tactics: (1) Engineer at least one explicit peak per session — celebration animation, micro-reward, witty copy moment, surprise progress reveal. Examples: confetti on lesson complete, a 'You saved 12 minutes' reveal, a personalized message. (2) Engineer the close: never let a session end on a friction event. Replace 'Are you sure?' modals on exit with 'Nice work today — see you tomorrow.' Use Reanimated 3 spring exit transitions, a final-state visual that captures the win (streak bumped, total earned, items completed). (3) Weekly/monthly wraps — Spotify Wrapped is the canonical reflective peak-end at the year scale. (4) Streak-save / forgiveness — when a peak is missed, design a 'streak repair' moment that itself becomes a peak (Duolingo's streak freeze + animation). (5) Onboarding curve — front-load a real win in the first session (the 'aha moment' framework, Sean Ellis), and end the onboarding with explicit confirmation of value. (6) Avoid mid-journey valleys: instrument session sentiment, identify negative peaks, fix them first. (7) Notifications themselves are mini-sessions — engineer their open-state to feel like a peak, not a guilt trip.
real_world_examples: (1) Duolingo end-of-lesson screen — Duo mascot celebration animation (Rive), XP burst, streak increment, immediate next-lesson CTA. The end-state IS the peak. (2) TurboTax filing-complete screen — illustration of relief, no upsell at the win moment (NN/g cite), big-screen confetti with shareable refund summary. (3) Headspace meditation session end — slow fade to a calming end-card with the day's insight; tomorrow's session preview (peak + soft close). (4) Spotify Wrapped — annual peak-end at scale; one of the most-shared product moments ever. (5) Apple Watch close-the-rings — three rings collapse into a celebration animation when all close; the haptic + visual at the end is the peak. (6) Strava 'Achievement Unlocked' end-of-run summary with PR badges. (7) Phantom Wallet successful-send screen — checkmark animation + transaction-share link as the peak ending. (8) Linear's task-complete satisfying check stroke — peak engineered at the per-task scale.
mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile has stronger peak-end leverage because of haptic + audio + screen-light at the moment of close. Use sensoryFeedback (.success) on iOS 17+, VibrationEffect.createPredefined(EFFECT_TICK) on Android, and ensure the close animation runs at 60+ fps. Web peaks rely on visual + audio only — confetti.js or Lottie can substitute; honor prefers-reduced-motion. Mobile sessions are also shorter and more frequent — peak-end engineering matters per session many times per day, vs. fewer-but-longer web sessions where in-session peaks have to be tracked harder.
platform_design_language_alignment: Aligned with both. Apple HIG 'Feedback' chapter explicitly recommends celebrating completion; Liquid Glass (iOS 26+) lends itself to expressive end-states. Material 3 Expressive's 18,000-participant research includes 'meaningful celebration moments' as a measured win factor. Ship-faster guidance: budget 10-15% of design effort on the close screen of every primary flow.
claude_design_application: Instruct Claude to engineer an explicit peak and close per flow. Example prompt fragment 1: 'For the workout-complete screen, design an explicit peak: full-bleed celebration with react-native-skia particle burst (24 particles, brand color #FF6B47, 700ms duration), Reanimated 3 spring scale-in for the achievement card (damping:14, stiffness:160), sensoryFeedback .success on iOS, VibrationEffect.EFFECT_HEAVY_CLICK on Android. Close state: shareable card with one-tap export, primary CTA See you tomorrow rather than another upsell. Forbid an upsell modal on this screen.' Example prompt fragment 2: 'Audit every primary user flow and identify (a) the peak moment (b) the close moment. Output a flow → peak → close table. For any flow missing one, propose a fix.'
stitch_application: Stitch must be told explicitly to design two states (peak + close) since it tends to default to a single end-screen. Example prompt fragment 1: 'Design TWO frames for the lesson-end flow. Frame 1 (PEAK): full-bleed celebration with custom-illustrated mascot, +20 XP burst centered, streak +1 flame icon top-right, bold 56pt rounded display type reading PERFECT (use DM Serif Display, NOT Inter). Frame 2 (CLOSE): calm muted end-card with the day's review summary, 24pt body type, primary CTA See you tomorrow, no upsell, dark mode #0E1116 background.' Example prompt fragment 2: 'Annual wrap-up screen — Spotify-Wrapped pattern. Vertical stack of 4 stat cards revealed sequentially, each in a different bold accent color (#FF6B47, #FFD93D, #6BCB77, #4D96FF). Final card is shareable with native share sheet CTA. Avoid generic confetti SVG — use a custom-illustrated motif unique to the brand.'
ai_codegen_failure_mode: AI codegen produces a generic toast Success! at the end, with no peak engineering. The end-screen is treated as plumbing, not as the most retention-leveraged moment in the product. Counter-prompt: 'The end-state is the most important screen of the flow — over-budget polish here. Forbid a generic Success! toast. Require: a custom celebration animation (Reanimated/Skia/Rive — not a stock Lottie), haptic + audio + visual triple-channel feedback, an explicit close state separate from the peak. Forbid an upsell modal on the success screen. Output a comment block listing the peak move and the close move before writing JSX.'
anti_patterns: (1) Upsell at the close — converting the peak moment into a 'Try Premium!' modal kills the positive memory and is on the dark-pattern radar (DFA, BEUC complaints 2025). (2) Flat session — no peak engineered, just a fade-out. Predicts higher D1 churn. (3) Ending on friction — error toast, sync conflict, or 'Are you sure?' modal as the last screen the user sees. (4) Front-loaded peaks only — celebration during onboarding then nothing for 30 days; users adapt and the peak becomes baseline (NN/g warning). (5) Manipulative streak-rescue — using the peak-end to instill guilt ('Don't lose your 47-day streak!') is on the regulatory hot zone. (6) Over-designed peaks — a 4-second confetti animation users want to skip after their fifth session.
regulatory_risk: Medium. Peak-end engineering is itself neutral, but two specific applications carry regulatory exposure: (1) streak-save / loss-aversion notifications targeting under-18 users — UK Age-Appropriate Design Code, California AADC, EU Digital Fairness Act (DFA, expected 2026) all flag streak-induced compulsion. BEUC's June 2025 SHEIN complaint cited dark-pattern engagement loops including loss-framed peaks. (2) Variable-reward peaks (slot-machine style randomized celebrations) draw scrutiny under DSA dark-pattern rules. Mitigate by giving users explicit control (streak freeze, opt-out of celebration animations) and avoiding loss-framed messaging.
pitfalls_caveats: (1) Adaptation — a celebration that fires every session becomes baseline within a week; rotate or scale up at milestones. (2) Cultural calibration — celebration register varies (loud/expressive in US, quieter in JP/KR; a Duolingo-level burst can feel embarrassing on a Tokyo subway). (3) Accessibility — celebrations must respect prefers-reduced-motion and be skippable; sound-on celebrations must respect mute. (4) Performance — heavy particle effects on low-end Android can drop frames during the most important moment of the flow. (5) Over-engineered peak overshadows the actual product value (NN/g caution).
cross_cultural_validity: Peak-end rule itself is biologically grounded (memory bias) and replicates broadly. Cultural calibration applies to expression intensity: US/EU markets tolerate exuberant celebration; Japanese, Korean, German users prefer restrained celebration (subtle haptic + small chime, not full-screen confetti). Microcopy register must localize — 'Crushing it!' translates poorly to most non-English markets. Streak-save and loss-aversion peaks should be softened in cultures with stronger collectivist norms where social shame is already heightened. RTL: celebration animations must mirror direction of progress (e.g., XP bar fills right-to-left in Arabic).
sources: [The Peak-End Rule: How Impressions Become Memories — Nielsen Norman Group (Lexie Kane, 2018)](https://www.nngroup.com/articles/peak-end-rule/)
[Kahneman, Fredrickson, Schreiber, Redelmeier (1993) — Adding a Better End](https://kenthendricks.com/peak-end-rule/)
[Frontiers in Psychology — Robustness of Peak-End Rule (2019)](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/psychology/articles/10.3389/fpsyg.2019.01705/full)
[A Review of the Peak-End Rule in Mental Health Contexts — PMC (2024)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11343653/)
